items.title: Basting stitch
items.description:
A basting stitch is a temporary stitch to hold layers of fabric together and it will later be removed.
Using a thread color that contrasts with your fabric will make the basting thread more visible for easy removal.

items.title: Running stitch
items.description:
The straight or running stitch is the basic stitch in hand-sewing and embroidery, on which all other forms of sewing are based. The stitch is worked by passing the needle in and out of the fabric at a regular distance.
Sew a line of running stitches by inserting the needle and thread through both layers of fabric a short distance from your starting point. Bring the needle and thread up to the top of the layers the same distance as the first stitch and keep repeating until you reach the end.

items.title: Back stitch
items.description:
A backstitch is a basic hand stitch that creates a line of stitches without a break between them, so it looks like a continuous straight line of thread, it is also one of the most durable stitches that doesn’t come undone easily.
The stitch is made by bringing thread behind the previous stitch and then coming out of the fabric in front of the previous stitch.

items.title: Chain stitch
items.description:
Chain stitch is a sewing and embroidery technique in which a series of looped stitches form a chain-like pattern.
Because of its continuous looped nature, the stitch easily comes apart so it is more suitable for decorative purposes than functional purposes like holding two or more fabric layers together.
Bring knotted needle to front of fabric. Pull thread through. Insert needle into fabric a tiny distance below where thread came out previously. Bring needle out from fabric to create a loop. Bring the needle to front a stitch length away from the starting point. Pull thread through to form first loop of the chain.
Continue forming loops in the same way.

items.title: Ladder stitch
items.description:
To sew a ladder stitch, start by threading a needle with matching thread and tying a small knot at the end. Begin by inserting the needle into one edge and sew a stitch then insert the needle into the other edge and sew a stitch with the same length, Continue alternating stitches between the two edges, making sure they are spaced evenly. Once you've reached the end, secure the thread with two or three knots and hide the thread tail inside the project.
The ladder stitch is also called the slip stitch or the invisible stitch or the blind stitch.

items.title: Blanket stitch
items.description:
A blanket stitch is a simple and decorative stitch often used for finishing edges of fabric or creating a neat hem.

1. Thread the needle and tie a small knot at the end.

2. Make a loop with the first stitch and pass needle under the loop over fabric edge as shown in the tutorial, I like to do this twice for a neat first stitch.

3. To sew the first stitch, insert the needle a stitch length away from the start point and slightly below the edge of the fabric, keeping the needle pointing up (towards fabric edge). Loop thread behind the needle and pull the needle through (up). This is your first blanket stitch.

items.title: Overlock stitch
items.description:
The overlock stitch is a variation of the blanket stitch. It’s both decorative and secure, making it perfect for finishing the edges of blankets, garments, and home textiles.

In this tutorial, I'll share my unique method for starting the stitch, which creates a perfect triangle as the first stitch. Let’s call it the 

To sew an overlock stitch by hand, use double thread. The stitches are formed by passing the needle between the threads and pulling the thread to tighten the loop, creating a triangle shape.

Repeat the steps until the edging is finished.

items.title: Catch stitch
items.description:
A catch stitch is a common hand sewing technique used for hemming, which creates a zigzag pattern on the underside of the fabric. It is strong and flexible, making it suitable for both non stretch and stretch fabrics, and is nearly invisible on the outside of the garment.

Before sewing the catch stitch, finish the raw fabric edges, then fold and press the hem at the seam allowance. Use a matching thread to sew the catch stitch.

A catch stitch can also be called a flat catch stitch. This technique involves taking a small amount of fabric from each side of the seam or hem, creating a zigzag pattern that lies flat against the fabric, resulting in a clean, professional finish.

items.title: Blind hem stitch
items.description:
A blind hem stitch is used to create a hem that is not visible once finished, It's quick and simple and it joins two pieces of fabric with practically invisible seams.

Use a matching thread and a single thread for lightweight fabrics and double thread for medium weight fabrics.

Fold the hem twice, insert the needle into the folded edge and hide the knot.
Take 1 or 2 thread from the wrong side and pull the needle through. Insert the needle into the folded edge and sew a small stitch.

Repeat until you reach the end of the hem. Tie the thread into the loop of the last stitch and hide the thread tail and trim the excess thread.

items.title: Buttonhole Stitch
items.description:
Draw a rectangle 2 mm × the length of the button  (this is the inner rectangle).

Next, draw a second rectangle so that each side is 2 mm away from the sides of the first rectangle (this will be the outer rectangle). 

Use a running stitch and sew along the edges of the outer rectangle to create a border for the buttonhole.

Then, fill the gap between the inner rectangle and the outer rectangle as shown in the videos.

Afterward, use a seam ripper to carefully open the buttonhole.

items.title: Start a stitch
items.description:
This is my go-to method for starting a stitch: 
Untwist the thread, tie a single knot at the end, and pass the needle through the loop formed by the double thread at the back (wrong side) before pulling it tight.

items.title: End a stitch
items.description:
Complete your final stitch and bring the needle to the WRONG SIDE of the fabric.
On the wrong side, pass the needle through the last stitch you made to create a small loop, and then pass the needle through the loop to form the first knot. Pull it tight to secure it.
Create a second knot by forming another small loop and passing the needle through it, then pulling it tight.
Once you're sure the knots are secure, trim any excess thread.

items.title: Hide the thread end #1
items.description:
How to hide the thread end after knotting and before cutting it off - Method #1
Depending on your project, you can choose this method, method #2 , or simply clip the thread after tying a knot at the end of the stitch.

items.title: Hide the thread end #2
items.description:
How to hide the thread end after knotting and before cutting it off  - Method #2

Insert the needle close to the knot, between the fabric layers away from the knot. Make sure the thread does not catch the outside of the fabric. Bring the needle up, away from the knot, and gently pull the thread to create a small gathering. Then, trim off the excess thread.

This technique effectively hides the thread ends, creating a clean, professional finish. 

Depending on your project, you can choose this method, Method #1, or simply clip the thread after tying a knot at the end of the stitch.

items.title: Open plain seam
items.description:
Sew the fabric pieces right sides together, finish the raw edges of the seam allowances separately, and press the seam open.
This technique is ideal for garments, quilting, and projects where a clean, flat seam is needed for a professional finish.

items.title: Closed plain seam
items.description:
Sew the fabric pieces right sides together, finish the raw edges of the seam allowances together, and press the seam to one side.

You can use this technique for garments like shirts, dresses, and pants to achieve a professional finish.

items.title: Edgestitch seam
items.description:
An edgestitch seam is a topstitch sewn close to the edge of the fabric, typically 1.5 to 3 mm away. It’s both decorative and functional, as it flattens seams and reinforces the edges.

To sew an edgestitch seam, use an edge stitching foot or the inside edge of the standard presser foot as a guide. With the right side of the fabric facing up, sew close to the seam, ensuring you catch the seam allowance on the wrong side.

Edgestitching is commonly used on garments to provide a clean finish. It’s also ideal for securing seam allowances in place, or for decorative accents in home décor items like pillowcases or tablecloths.

items.title: Topstitch seam
items.description:
A topstitch seam is a decorative or functional stitching line sewn on the right side of a garment, parallel to a seam.
It is often used to reinforce seams, add definition, or create a finished look.
Topstitching is usually sewn about 6mm (or 1/4 inch) away from the seam line. You can also use the outer edge of your standard presser foot as a guide.

items.title: Welt Seam
items.description:
A welt seam is a strong, flat seam often used for medium to heavy-weight fabrics. It resembles a flat-felled seam but is less bulky, making it ideal for jeans, denim, and other garments that require durability.

To sew a welt seam:
Place the fabric right sides together and sew the seam, finish the raw edges together using a serger or zigzag stitch, press the seam allowances to one side, with the right side facing up, sew an edgestitch close to the seam, then add a topstitch.

Tip: You can trim one seam allowance to reduce bulk if needed.

items.title: French seam
items.description:
Sew the pieces wrong sides together with a 4–5 mm seam allowance (You can also sew with a wider seam allowance and then trim it to 4–5 mm). Press the seam allowance to one side.
Fold the fabric along the seam so right sides are together, and press again.
Sew a second seam with a 7–8 mm seam allowance, enclosing the raw edge inside.

A French seam is a sewing technique often used for lightweight or sheer fabrics. It creates a clean, durable, and neat finish by enclosing the raw edges within the seam, resulting in a smooth, professional look on both sides of the garment.

items.title: Flat Felled Seam
items.description:
Place the pieces right sides together. One seam allowance is 1 cm, and the other is 2 cm.
Fold the wider seam allowance over the narrower one and sew a row of stitches close to the raw edge. Then Fold the entire seam allowance to one side so the raw edge is hidden inside.
After pressing, sew an edgestitch and a topstitch from the right side, making sure to catch the folded seam allowance underneath.
Press and done!

A flat felled seam is a super strong, and professional looking seam inside and out, often used in jeans and shirts. It’s durable and leaves no raw edges, perfect for garments that need strength and a neat finish!

items.title: Two-hole button #1
items.description:
Thread the cut ends or the looped end into the eye of the needle, making sure the folded end is at the bottom.

Insert the needle from the wrong side to the right side of the fabric, passing it through one hole of the button—but don’t pull it all the way through. Leave a small loop on the wrong side.

Now, bring the needle from the right side to the wrong side through the second hole of the button. Pass the needle and thread through the loop on the wrong side, then pull to tighten it.

This method helps you avoid knots on the back of your project.

To complete sewing the two-hole button, watch the next parts in this series:
#2 two-hole button, how to sew 
#3 two-hole button, how to finish

items.title: Two-hole button #2
items.description:
This is part two, don't forget to watch part one #1 how to start sewing a two-hole button on my account.

After starting, use a thick needle and place it between the first stitch and the button to create a small space between the button and the fabric. This space is essential — it helps the button sit perfectly when it’s closed into the buttonhole.
Sew three times using a double-threaded needle, and we’re ready to finish it.

Watch the next part to learn how to finish:
#3 two-hole button, how to finish

items.title: Two-hole button #3
items.description:
This is part three, don't forget to watch part one #1 how to start sewing a two-hole button, and part two #2 how to sew a two-hole button on my account.

After sewing the button in place, finish it on the wrong side of the garment: Pass the needle through a stitch to make a loop, then pass the needle through the loop and pull to tighten. Repeat one more time to make the knots secure. Then, pass the needle between the fabric layers, push the fabric down along the thread with your fingers, and cut it short. Straighten the fabric — the small thread tail will be hidden inside, invisible.

Now it’s time to remove the thick needle… and done! You just sewed the most perfect button!

items.title: 3 Tips to Sew a Button
items.description:
3 Tips to Sew a Button with a Clean Finish on Both Sides:
1. Start without a knot — you can watch the “Start Without a Knot” tutorial on my account.
2. Keep an eye on where you insert the needle.
3. Hide the thread end after knotting it off.

How to sew a button #1 how to start sewing a four-hole button 
To complete sewing the four-hole button, watch the next parts in this series:
